content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 9, 2010


Chris Reynolds
Acting Chief
Political Reform Division
Secretary of State
1500 11th Street, 6th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-131

Dear Mr. Reynolds:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Online Disclosure Act, added to the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ in 1997. We base this letter on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does Section 84605(g) require entities that file campaign statements electronically on a voluntary basis to continue to file electronically/online with the Secretary of State?

CONCLUSION

Section 84605(g) requires a person or entity that is required to file online or electronically to file all subsequent reports online or electronically. At this time, persons who merely file online or electronically on a volunteer basis are not required to file subsequent reports online or electronically pursuant to Section 84605(g). However, this analysis could change if the Commission adopted a regulation clarifying the applicable statute.

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

~*~

File No. A-10-131
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

The Act requires certain persons who file campaign reports with the Secretary of State to
file these reports online or electronically once they surpass a threshold of $50,000 in activity.
Persons required to file electronically are:

• Any candidate, including appellate court and Supreme Court candidates and officeholders,
committees, or other persons who are required to file statements, reports, or other documents
in connection with a state elective office or state measure, provided that the total cumulative
reportable amount of contributions received, expenditures made, loans made, or loans
received is $50,000 or more. In determining the cumulative reportable amount, all controlled
committees, as defined by Section 82016, shall be included. A committee, as defined in
subdivision (c) of Section 82013, shall file online or electronically if it makes contributions
of $50,000 or more in a calendar year.2 (Section 84605(a).)

• Any general purpose committees, as defined in Section 82027.5, including the general
purpose committees of political parties, and small contributor committees, as defined in
Section 85203, that cumulatively receive contributions or make expenditures totaling
$50,000 or more to support or oppose candidates for any elective state office or state
measure. For a committee subject to this title prior to January 1, 2000, the beginning date for
calculating cumulative totals is January 1, 2000. For a committee that first is subject to this
title on or after January 1, 2000, the beginning date for calculating cumulative totals is the
date the committee is first subject to this title. (Section 84605(b).)

• Any slate mailer organization with cumulative reportable payments received or made for the
purposes of producing slate mailers of $50,000 or more. For a slate mailer organization
subject to this title prior to January 1, 2000, the beginning date for calculating cumulative
totals is January 1, 2000. For a slate mailer organization that first is subject to this title on or
after January 1, 2000, the beginning date for calculating cumulative totals is the date the
organization is first subject to this title. (Section 84605(c).)

• Any lobbyist, lobbying firm, lobbyist employer or other persons required, pursuant to
Chapter 6 (commencing with Section 86100), to file statements, reports, or other documents,
provided that the total amount of any category of reportable payments, expenses,
contributions, gifts, or other items is $5,000 or more in a calendar quarter. (Section
84605(d).)

• The Secretary of State shall also disclose on the Internet any late contribution or late
independent expenditure report, as defined by Sections 84203 and 84204, respectively, not
covered by subdivision (a), (b), or (c). (Section 84605(e).)

_______________________________
2 For a committee subject to this title prior to January 1, 2000, the beginning date for calculating
cumulative totals is January 1, 2000. For a committee that first is subject to this title on or after January 1, 2000, the
beginning date for calculating cumulative totals is the date the committee is first subject to this title.

File No. A-10-131
Page No. 3

• Section 84605(f) provides: "Committees and other persons that are not required to file online or electronically by this section may do so voluntarily." (Section 84605(f), emphasis added.)

• Finally, once a person or entity is required to file online or electronically, subject to subdivision (a), (b), (c), (d), or (f), the person or entity shall be required to file all subsequent reports online or electronically." (Section 84605(g), emphasis added.)

Your question specifically concerns persons that are not required to file electronically under any of the statutory provisions of Section 84605, but rather these persons file voluntarily as permitted under Section 84605(f).

In prior advice letters concerning Section 84605, we have not had the opportunity to address the interplay of subdivisions (f) and (g). Subdivision (g) of the statute explicitly lists persons "required" to file under subdivision (f) as those that shall be required to file all subsequent reports online or electronically. However, subdivision (f) does not "require" anyone to file under the Act. In fact, subdivision (f) explicitly applies only to committees and other persons that are not required to file online or electronically. Subdivision (f) merely allows a person to file online or electronically voluntarily when not required to do so by any other provision in the statute.

Legislative History: Section 84605 was enacted in 1997 (as SB 49) as part of the Online Disclosure Act. During the consideration of the bill, the various analyses' of the Assembly and Senate stated that the drafters intended that voluntary filers become mandatory filers. For example, the Senate Rules Committee Senate Floor Analysis of SB 49 (September 8, 1997)³ stated: "This bill specifies that any entity not mandated to file on-line may do so voluntarily. Once an entity, whether by mandate or choice, files required reports on-line, all subsequent reports must also be filed on-line." However, the chaptered bill language of subdivision (g), which imposes the ongoing mandatory online filing, did not reference subdivision (f). It read: "Once a person or entity is required to file online, subject to subdivision (a), (b), (c), or (d), they shall be required to file all subsequent reports online." Thus, while the legislative intent supported the requirement that that voluntary filers being required to report online thereafter, the legislature never amended the language to reflect the requirement.

In 1999, the Online Disclosure Act was once again subject to revision. In 1999, SB 658 (Karnette) was introduced to make necessary changes to the Online Disclosure Act of 1997, which required the Secretary of State to develop an online filing process for purposes of filing statements and reports with the Secretary of State. The bill amended subdivision (g) to add a reference to subdivision (f) in to subdivision (g).⁴ However, none of the Legislative analyses of the bill explained the purpose or intent behind the amendment to subdivision (g).

³ See also the analysis for the Assembly Committee on Elections, Reapportionment and Constitutional Amendments hearing of July 14, 1997; Senate Floor Analyses' (May 30,1997 and June 3, (997) and the Senate Committee on Elections and Reapportionment analysis for April 16, 1997.

⁴ The language change was not in the introduced version of the bill, but was amended into the bill on July 6, 1999.

File No. A-10-131
Page No. 4

Thus, in spite of the 1999 amendment to subdivision (g) (even in light of the explicit
legislative intent expressed in the 1997 bill analyses), the statute itself is ambiguous on this
point. Therefore, absent a regulation harmonizing these provisions and effectuating the
legislative intent, we do not interpret subdivision (g) to apply to filers under subdivision (f) at
this time. Consequently, until the Commission acts to adopt a regulation saying otherwise,
voluntarily filing under subdivision (f) does not create an obligation on the part of the filer to
continue filing online or electronically.

Please note, the Governor has signed legislation, AB 1181 (Chapter 18, Statutes of 2010),
which will change the filing requirements of Section 84215. The bill would lower the electronic
filing threshold from $50,000 to $25,000. Thus, many persons not required to file online or
electronically in the past will be required to file in the future. The advice in this letter is limited
to those persons who are not required to file under any section of Section 84605, either as
originally enacted or as amended.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
